core_concept: World Biomes: Tropical Rainforest, Desert, Grassland, Deciduous Forest, Boreal Forest, and Tundra
facts: A tropical rainforest has a hot and wet climate all year, which allows many different kinds of plants and animals to live there, unlike a desert which is very dry.
The tundra has permafrost, a layer of frozen soil that stays frozen all year, so trees cannot grow there because their roots cannot reach deep into the ground.
A desert gets less than 25 centimeters of rain per year, so animals like camels have adaptations such as storing water to survive the dry conditions.
Temperate deciduous forests have trees that lose their leaves in winter, while boreal forests (taiga) have evergreen trees that keep their needles all year because the growing season is short.
misconceptions: Students think all forests are the same, but a tropical rainforest has no cold season and very different plants and animals than a deciduous forest or taiga.
Students believe the tundra is just a very cold desert with no life, but it actually has many plants like mosses and animals like caribou that are adapted to the cold and permafrost.
